quests: Psyche (Soul): Represents the human soul's journey toward divine love
Eros (Love): Divine love that transforms and elevates
The Trials: The soul's purification through suffering and perseverance
Looking at Eros: The danger of curiosity and lack of faith
Apotheosis: The soul achieving immortality through love

companions.name: Zeus
companions.description: King of the Gods, who ultimately grants Psyche immortality, solidifying her union with Eros.

companions.name: Gaia
companions.description: The Earth goddess, who provides Psyche with crucial advice and assistance during her trials.

companions.name: Ants
companions.description: A colony of ants that assist Psyche in sorting a massive pile of grains, demonstrating the power of humility and aid from unexpected sources.

name: Eros and Psyche

weapons.name: Lamp of Curiosity
weapons.description: Used by Psyche to unveil Eros's true form, symbolizing the dangerous pursuit of forbidden knowledge.

weapons.name: Box of Persephone
weapons.description: A container holding a sleep-inducing substance, representing the perils of temptation and the soul's vulnerability.

weapons.name: Golden Fleece
weapons.description: Wool taken from dangerous sheep, representing a challenge of courage and resourcefulness.

description:
Discover the legendary heroes, warriors, and mortal champions.

keyMyths.title: Eros Wounds Himself and Falls in Love
keyMyths.description: Eros, tasked by Aphrodite to make Psyche fall in love with a monster, accidentally wounds himself with his own arrow and falls deeply in love with her.

keyMyths.title: Psyche's Forbidden Marriage
keyMyths.description: Psyche is taken to a hidden palace and marries Eros, who remains unseen and forbids her from looking upon his face, threatening to end their relationship if she disobeys.

keyMyths.title: The Sisters' Treachery
keyMyths.description: Psyche's jealous sisters convince her that her husband is a monster and urge her to kill him, leading her to betray his trust and attempt to murder him.

keyMyths.title: Psyche's Four Labors for Aphrodite
keyMyths.description: To win back Eros, Psyche performs four impossible tasks set by Aphrodite: sorting a mountain of mixed grains, gathering golden fleece from vicious sheep, collecting water from the River Styx, and retrieving a box of beauty from Persephone.

keyMyths.title: Psyche Opens Persephone's Box and Falls into a Deep Sleep
keyMyths.description: Driven by curiosity, Psyche opens the box from Persephone, hoping to enhance her own beauty for Eros, but instead falls into a death-like sleep, from which only Eros can awaken her.

achievements: Married a god (Eros) despite being mortal.
Maintained love for Eros despite never seeing his face initially.
Resisted the temptation to reveal Eros's identity for a significant period.
Completed four impossible labors set by Aphrodite.
Traveled to the Underworld and back.
Overcame the machinations of jealous sisters and the wrath of a goddess (Aphrodite).

sources.name: Apuleius, The Golden Ass (Metamorphoses)
sources.description: The most complete and detailed account of the myth of Eros and Psyche.
sources.title: Apuleius, The Golden Ass (Metamorphoses)

sources.name: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.description: Provides background on the lineage of the gods, including Aphrodite and Eros.
sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony

sources.name: Plato, Symposium
sources.description: Offers philosophical interpretations of Eros and love.
sources.title: Plato, Symposium

sources.name: Ovid, Metamorphoses
sources.description: Brief allusions to the themes present in the Eros and Psyche myth.
sources.title: Ovid, Metamorphoses

sources.name: Graves, Robert. The Greek Myths
sources.description: A modern retelling and analysis of Greek myths, including Eros and Psyche.
sources.title: Graves, Robert. The Greek Myths

sources.name: Hamilton, Edith. Mythology: Timeless Tales of Gods and Heroes
sources.description: A popular introduction to Greek mythology, including a summary of the Eros and Psyche story.
sources.title: Hamilton, Edith. Mythology: Timeless Tales of Gods and Heroes

associations.name: Rose
associations.significance: Represents love, beauty, and the blossoming of Psyche's soul.

associations.name: Butterfly
associations.significance: Symbolizes Psyche's soul and transformation, emerging from a mortal state to divine status.

associations.name: Lamp
associations.significance: Represents Psyche's curiosity and the revealing of hidden truths, despite the risks.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Associated with Psyche's purification and trials, often involving perilous journeys across water.

associations.name: Jealousy
associations.significance: Embodies the envy of Aphrodite, highlighting the challenges Psyche faces due to her beauty.

associations.name: Perseverance
associations.significance: Reflects Psyche's unwavering determination to overcome obstacles and reunite with Eros.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A mortal woman and a divine being united in love, facing trials and tribulations.
A celestial nymph and a mortal king whose love is tested by divine conditions and separation.
Star-crossed lovers separated by celestial boundaries, allowed to meet only once a year.

worshipPractices.name: Household Shrines
worshipPractices.description: Small altars dedicated to Eros and Psyche in homes, symbolizing the importance of love and the soul's journey in domestic life. Offerings included flowers and incense.

worshipPractices.name: Festivals of Love
worshipPractices.description: Celebrations honoring the union of Eros and Psyche, emphasizing the triumph of love over adversity and the soul's attainment of immortality. These festivals often involved theatrical performances and symbolic rituals.

cultural.modernLegacy: The tale of Eros and Psyche continues to inspire art, literature, and psychology, representing the journey of love, self-discovery, and the soul's transformation. The story's themes resonate in contemporary narratives exploring relationships, identity, and personal growth.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Psyche's Beauty
extendedContent.content: Psyche, a mortal princess, was renowned for her unparalleled beauty, surpassing even that of Aphrodite, the goddess of love. This ignited Aphrodite's jealousy, leading her to task her son, Eros, to make Psyche fall in love with a hideous monster. However, Eros was so struck by Psyche's beauty that he disobeyed his mother and instead, made her his wife. He kept his identity hidden, visiting her only at night and forbidding her from ever looking upon his face. Psyche lived in a magnificent palace, attended by invisible servants, and enjoyed every pleasure, but she longed for her family and was plagued by curiosity about her husband's true form. Her sisters, driven by envy, planted seeds of doubt in her mind, leading her to believe that she was married to a terrible serpent. This sets the stage for Psyche's journey of self-discovery and the trials she must endure to prove her love and worthiness.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Trials
extendedContent.content: Driven by curiosity and the manipulation of her sisters, Psyche disobeyed Eros's command and gazed upon his divine form while he slept. A drop of hot oil from her lamp awakened him, and he fled, heartbroken by her betrayal. Realizing her mistake, Psyche embarked on a perilous quest to win back her love. She sought out Aphrodite, who, still consumed by jealousy, imposed a series of seemingly impossible tasks. These included sorting an enormous pile of mixed grains, gathering golden fleece from dangerous sheep, collecting water from the River Styx, and retrieving a box of Persephone's beauty. With the help of various gods and creatures, Psyche completed each task, demonstrating her resilience, humility, and resourcefulness. Her journey symbolizes the trials and tribulations one must face to achieve true love and self-realization. Each task forced her to confront her fears and weaknesses.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Immortality
extendedContent.content: Having successfully completed Aphrodite's trials, Psyche's final task led her to open the box of Persephone's beauty, hoping to enhance her own allure for Eros. However, the box contained not beauty but a deep sleep, which overcame her. Eros, having recovered from his initial heartbreak, found Psyche in this state and awakened her with a kiss. He then appealed to Zeus, the king of the gods, for his blessing. Zeus, moved by their love and Psyche's unwavering devotion, granted her immortality, making her a goddess and forever uniting her with Eros. Psyche's transformation from a mortal princess to a goddess symbolizes the soul's journey towards enlightenment and the attainment of divine status through love and perseverance. Their union represents the harmonious balance between love and soul, passion and intellect, and the eternal bond between the divine and the mortal.

shortDescription: A mortal princess, Psyche's beauty ignited Aphrodite's jealousy. Her love for Eros led her through trials, ultimately achieving immortality and uniting love and soul.
subtitle: A tale of love, trials, and immortal union
